questions.question_text: What distinguishes a broker associate from a standard broker in North Dakota?
questions.correct_answer: A broker associate works under another broker
distractors: A broker associate cannot supervise salespersons
A broker associate holds a salesperson license
A broker associate has fewer education hours
questions.explanation: A broker associate holds a broker license but chooses to work under the supervision of another designated broker rather than operating independently.

questions.question_text: A salesperson wants to upgrade to a broker license. Beyond passing the broker exam, what additional requirements must be met?
questions.correct_answer: 2 years experience and 60 additional hours
questions.explanation: To qualify for a broker license in North Dakota, a salesperson must have at least 2 years of active experience and complete 60 additional hours of approved education beyond the salesperson requirement.

questions.question_text: Which license type is considered the entry-level real estate license in North Dakota?
questions.correct_answer: Salesperson license
questions.explanation: The salesperson license is the entry-level license in North Dakota. Salespersons must work under the supervision of a licensed broker.

questions.question_text: A newly licensed broker decides she does not want to open her own firm. She joins an existing brokerage instead. What is her license classification?
questions.correct_answer: Broker associate
questions.explanation: A broker who holds a broker license but works under another designated broker is classified as a broker associate in North Dakota.

questions.question_text: Tom has been actively licensed as a salesperson for 18 months and completed 60 additional education hours. Can he apply for a broker license?
questions.correct_answer: No, he needs at least 2 years of experience
distractors: No, he needs 90 additional education hours
Yes, if his broker writes a recommendation
questions.explanation: North Dakota requires 2 years of active salesperson experience before upgrading to a broker license. Tom's 18 months falls short of this requirement, even though he has completed the education.

questions.question_text: Maria, age 19, completed 90 hours of pre-license education 18 months ago. She scored 68% national and 78% state on the PSI exam. Can she obtain a salesperson license?
questions.correct_answer: No, she failed the national portion
distractors: No, she is too young to apply
questions.explanation: The national portion of the PSI exam requires a minimum score of 70%, and the state portion requires 75%. Maria's 68% national score falls below the 70% threshold, so she cannot be licensed until she retakes and passes the national portion.

questions.question_text: Which item is required for a North Dakota salesperson license application?
questions.correct_answer: Criminal background check with fingerprints
questions.explanation: North Dakota salesperson applicants must submit to a criminal background check with fingerprinting as part of the application process, along with meeting education, exam, E&O insurance, and broker affiliation requirements.

questions.question_text: Jake completed his 90-hour pre-license course on March 1, 2024. By what date must he apply for his license?
questions.correct_answer: March 1, 2026
questions.explanation: Applicants must apply for their North Dakota real estate license within 2 years of completing the pre-license education course. Jake has until March 1, 2026.

questions.question_text: What is the total initial fee a new salesperson pays when applying for a North Dakota license?
questions.correct_answer: $170 total
questions.explanation: New salesperson applicants pay a $150 application fee plus a one-time $20 Recovery Fund fee, totaling $170.

questions.question_text: An applicant passes the PSI exam with 72% national and 74% state scores. What is the result?
questions.correct_answer: The applicant failed the state portion
distractors: The applicant must retake the entire exam
questions.explanation: North Dakota requires 70% on the national portion and 75% on the state portion. The applicant passed national (72%) but failed state (74%, below the 75% threshold) and must retake the state portion.

questions.question_text: A licensed broker from Minnesota wants to practice in North Dakota. Under reciprocity, what is waived?
questions.correct_answer: The licensing examination
questions.explanation: North Dakota has reciprocity agreements with Minnesota, Iowa, and Georgia. Licensees from these states who are in good standing and primarily reside there may have the exam requirement waived.

questions.question_text: A salesperson licensed in Texas wants to obtain a North Dakota license. Texas is NOT a reciprocal state. What must the applicant do?
questions.correct_answer: Pass the North Dakota state exam
distractors: Obtain a letter from the Texas commission
questions.explanation: North Dakota has reciprocity only with MN, IA, and GA. Licensees from non-reciprocal states like Texas must pass the North Dakota state portion of the licensing exam.

questions.question_text: Which states have reciprocity agreements with North Dakota for real estate licensing?
questions.correct_answer: Minnesota, Iowa, and Georgia
questions.explanation: North Dakota maintains reciprocity agreements with only three states: Minnesota, Iowa, and Georgia. Licensees from these states may have exam requirements waived if they are in good standing and primarily reside in the reciprocal state.

questions.question_text: An Iowa broker wants to use reciprocity to get a North Dakota license but moved to Nebraska last year. Is she eligible?
questions.correct_answer: No, she must reside in the reciprocal state
distractors: Yes, her Iowa license is still active
Yes, if she renews her Iowa license first
No, but she can use Nebraska reciprocity
questions.explanation: North Dakota reciprocity requires that the applicant primarily reside in the reciprocal state. Since the broker moved to Nebraska, which is not a reciprocal state, she no longer qualifies for the exam waiver.

questions.question_text: A Georgia salesperson applies under reciprocity. Which requirement is NOT waived?
questions.correct_answer: The E&O insurance requirement
questions.explanation: Reciprocity with Georgia waives the exam requirement, but the applicant must still meet all other North Dakota requirements including E&O insurance, background check, and application fees.

questions.question_text: A salesperson leaves Broker A to join Broker B. What must happen regarding the license?
questions.correct_answer: The license must be transferred to Broker B
distractors: The salesperson must retake the state exam
A new application and fee are required
questions.explanation: When a salesperson changes employing brokers, the license must be formally transferred. The salesperson does not need to retake any exam or submit a new full application.

questions.question_text: A salesperson decides to stop practicing and places her license on inactive status. What must she do to resume practice later?
questions.correct_answer: Apply for reinstatement with an active broker
distractors: Retake and pass the licensing exam
Complete 90 hours of pre-license education again
questions.explanation: A licensee on inactive status must apply for reinstatement and affiliate with an active broker to resume practicing. The specific reinstatement requirements depend on how long the license has been inactive.

questions.question_text: Broker A closes his firm. His three salespersons have not yet found new brokers. What is the status of their licenses?
questions.correct_answer: Their licenses become inactive
distractors: They may practice independently for 30 days
Their licenses transfer to the NDREC
questions.explanation: Salespersons must be affiliated with an active broker to hold an active license. When their broker closes the firm, their licenses become inactive until they transfer to a new broker.

questions.question_text: A licensee has been on inactive status for six months and wants to reactivate. During inactive status, what was she prohibited from doing?
questions.correct_answer: Engaging in any real estate activities
questions.explanation: An inactive licensee cannot engage in any real estate brokerage activities, including listing, selling, or negotiating transactions. The license must be reactivated through proper channels before resuming practice.

questions.question_text: A salesperson transfers from Broker A to Broker B on March 15. Who notifies the NDREC of the transfer?
questions.correct_answer: Both the releasing and receiving brokers
distractors: Only the new broker receiving the license
questions.explanation: When a salesperson transfers between brokers, both the releasing broker and the receiving broker must notify the NDREC to update the license records and ensure proper supervision.
